coredata.dc:title: An ultrasensitive sensing platform based on fluorescence carbon dots for chlorogenic acid determination in food samples
coredata.dc:creator: Hu, Qin
Wang, Wenhui
Yang, Zhen-quan
Xiao, Lixia
Gong, Xiaojuan
Liu, Lizhen
Han, Jie
coredata.dc:description: In this work, an ultrasensitive and convenient method was established for chlorogenic acid (CGA) determination based on fluorescence quenching of carbon dots (CDs). The CDs were prepared by hydrothermal heating of citric acid and p-phenylenediamine. The proposed CDs-based sensing platform showed high selectivity and sensitivity towards CGA detection. Under optimal working conditions, the fluorescence signals of CDs decreased with increasing of CGA contents and presented linear response to CGA content in two ranges of 0.01–0.1 and 0.1–20.0 μM. The detection limits were as low as 8.87 nM and 0.12 μM. The proposed method was successfully applied to analyze CGA in real food samples. The recoveries were between 98.9 % and 106.7 % and the relative standard deviations (RSDs) were below 3.28 %. This work highlights the construction of a facial, simple, economic and highly sensitive fluorescence sensing system for CGA detection with promising application prospects in food analysis.
coredata.dcterms:subject: CDs
Fluorescence quenching
CGA
Food samples

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: CGA determination in food samples by HPLC analysis
Optical and structural characterization of CDs
Fluorescence quenching of CDs by CGA
Response mechanism of CDs to CGA

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) The TEM image of the as-prepared CDs. The insert is the corresponding particle size distribution histogram. (B) The UV–vis absorption spectrum (Abs) and the excitation (Ex) and emission (Em) spectra of CDs. The insert is the partial enlargement of UV–vis absorption spectrum of CDs in a range of 320–420 nm. (C) The emission spectra of CDs under the excitation wavelength varying from 310 to 450 nm.

(A) The fluorescence spectra of CDs in the presence of different concentrations of CGA (1–20: 0.0, 0.08, 0.2, 1.0, 3.0, 5.0, 6.0, 8.0, 11.0, 14.0, 17.0, 20.0, 24.0, 28.0, 34.0, 40.0, 50.0, 60.0, 80.0 and 110.0 µM). (B) and (C) are linear plots of F0/F against CGA concentration. The error bars indicate the standard deviations from five independent measurements (n = 5).

(A) The UV–vis absorption spectrum of CGA (solid red line) and the excitation (black dotted line) and emission (blue dotted line) spectra of CDs. (B) The fluorescence decay curve of CDs (black line) and the CDs/CGA system (red line). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(A) The F0/F of CDs in the presence of CGA and various possible interfering substances (cations, anions, CGA isomers and the other small molecules). (B) The F0/F of the CDs/CGA system in the presence of various possible interfering substances. The error bars are the standard deviations calculated from five independent experiments (n = 5).
Schematic illustration of the synthesis of CDs and the detection principle for CGA.
The CGA detection results in food samples by the proposed CDs-based fluorescence approach and HPLC.

head.ce:title: An ultrasensitive sensing platform based on fluorescence carbon dots for chlorogenic acid determination in food samples

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Food Science and Engineering, Yangzhou University, Yangzhou, Jiangsu 225001, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Food Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Food Science and Engineering, Yangzhou University, Yangzhou, Jiangsu 225001, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Environmental Science, and School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shanxi University, Taiyuan 030006, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Environmental Science, and School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute of Environmental Science, and School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shanxi University, Taiyuan 030006, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Environmental Engineering, Shanxi Datong University, Datong 037009, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Environmental Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Environmental Engineering, Shanxi Datong University, Datong 037009, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Yangzhou University, Yangzhou, Jiangsu, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Yangzhou University, Yangzhou, Jiangsu, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, an ultrasensitive and convenient method was established for chlorogenic acid (CGA) determination based on fluorescence quenching of carbon dots (CDs). The CDs were prepared by hydrothermal heating of citric acid and p-phenylenediamine. The proposed CDs-based sensing platform showed high selectivity and sensitivity towards CGA detection. Under optimal working conditions, the fluorescence signals of CDs decreased with increasing of CGA contents and presented linear response to CGA content in two ranges of 0.01–0.1 and 0.1–20.0 μM. The detection limits were as low as 8.87 nM and 0.12 μM. The proposed method was successfully applied to analyze CGA in real food samples. The recoveries were between 98.9 % and 106.7 % and the relative standard deviations (RSDs) were below 3.28 %. This work highlights the construction of a facial, simple, economic and highly sensitive fluorescence sensing system for CGA detection with promising application prospects in food analysis.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The phenolic compounds, which originate from cinnamic and benzoic acids, play an important role in growth regulation and plant resistance (Coelho et al., 2014). Chlorogenic acid (CGA) is a kind of phenylpropanoid compound that widely exist in beverages and various natural foods such as fruits and vegetables (Alpar, Yardım, & Şentürk, 2018). The CGA was reported to be a bioactive component with attractive functions of antioxidative, antidiabetic, antiobesity, antibacterial, anti-inflammatory and anti-cancer properties (Cho et al., 2010; Pal, Mohiyuddin, & Packirisamy, 2018; Sato et al., 2011; Shin et al., 2015). It can also increase white blood cells and excite central nervous system (Hemmerle et al., 1997). On account of this, researches on investigating the CGA bioactivity have been widely carried out, which drives the emergency of various analytical methods for CGA detection.

The traditional analytical methods for CGA analysis include thin-layer scanning (Lebot, Michalet, & Legendre, 2016), micellar electrokinetic chromatography (Risso, Péres, & Amaya-Farfan, 2007), high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) (Che et al., 2016; Craig, Fields, Liang, Kitts, & Erickson, 2016), liquid chromatography tandem mass spectrometry (LC-MS/MS) (Crupi et al., 2018; Jaiswal, Muller, Muller, Karar, & Kuhnert, 2014) and electrochemical method (Santos Wde, Santhiago, Yoshida, & Kubota, 2011; Zhang, Chen, Huang, Wang, & Hu, 2018). However, most of these approaches require expensive instrumentation and suffer from complex operation, long measurement time and low efficiency, which limit their practical application scope. Recently, the fluorescence methods especially the fluorescence nanoprobes (Liu et al., 2021; Yang et al., 2018) stand out and received much attention due to their numerous appealing merits in rapidity, simplicity, low cost, favorable sensitivity and high convenience.

Carbon dots (CDs) are a type of zero-dimensional fluorescent nanomaterials with high oxygen content and sizes below 10 nm (Rahimi, Mahani, & Hassani, 2019; Wang, Lin, & Huang, 2019; Xu et al., 2018). Due to the quantum confinement and edge effects, the CDs enjoy the signature features like tunable emission, excitation-wavelength (λex) dependent photoluminescence (PL) behavior, excellent water solubility and superior photo-stability (Li, Kang, Liu, & Lee, 2012; Lim, Shen, & Gao, 2015; Liu et al., 2021). These advantages make them favorable nanomaterials in various application fields, especially serving as fluorescence nanoprobe for small molecules detection. Numerous literature evidence have shown that the CDs nanoprobe exhibit representing advancement in high selectivity and sensitivity for food composition or food additives detection, such as curcumin (Hu et al., 2019), glutathione (Hu et al., 2020), folic acid (Zhan, Wang, & Yu, 2022), sunset yellow (Liu et al., 2021), carmine (Hu et al., 2021), etc. However, there are seldom reports on application of CDs for CGA analysis, and so far only two reports can be found. For instance, Zhu et al. reported a CDs-based nanoprobe from hydrothermal heating of citric acid and l-histidine for CGA detection via inner filter effect (IFE), achieving a limit of detection (LOD) of 0.46 μM (Han, He, Pan, Liu, & Hu, 2020). Liu et al. proposed a ratiometric fluorescence method based on CDs from hydrothermal heating of citric acid/sulfamic acid and polyethyleneimine (PEI) with a LOD of 0.34 μM (0.12 μg/mL) was achieved (Liu et al., 2021). Since the analytical performance still has large room to improve, it is of high necessity to explore more efficient CDs-based nanoprobe for CGA detection.

In this study, a rapid and facial fluorescence method based on CDs was developed for CGA detection (Scheme 1 ) with the purpose of achieving enhanced sensitivity. The CDs was simply prepared by hydrothermal heating of citric acid and p-phenylenediamine. The CDs exhibited bright yellow fluorescence under UV irradiation, the intensity of which was efficiently quenched by CGA at a concentration-dependent manner via IFE and dynamic quenching. Based on this observation, we hypothesize that the CDs could serve as an effective fluorescence nanoprobe for CGA detection with high sensitivity. Ultimately, the as-constructed method was applied for the detection of CGA in foodstuffs with satisfying results.

The chlorogenic acid and isochlorogenic acid A was purchased from Shanghai Yuanye Biotechnology Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). The p-phenylenediamine (p-pda), Cynarin, Na3PO4, Na2HPO4 and NaH2PO4 were obtained from Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). The citric acid, fructose, sucrose, glucose and all amino acids were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). The folic acid, ascorbic acid, all inorganic salts, anhydrous ethanol, acetonitrile and ammonium acetate were purchased from Sangon Biotech Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). The neochlorogenic acid, cryptochlorogenic acid, isochlorogenic acid B and isochlorogenic acid C were from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). The The instant coffee, green coffee beans and roasted beans were bought from Nestle Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China), Lvzhisu Co., Ltd (Yunnan, China) and Sinloy Co., Ltd (Yunnan, China), respectively. The honeysuckle was bought from the local markets at Yangzhou, Jiangsu province, China. The ultrapure water was purified through a Millipore-Q system (Bedford, MA, USA) and used throughout the experiments. All chemicals and reagents are of analytical grade or above and do not require further purification prior to use. The purity and/or detail product code of all chemicals, reagents and samples were deposited in Supplementary Material (Table S1).

First, 0.36 g citric acid and 0.1 g p-pda were dissolved in 20.0 mL of a mixture of anhydrous ethanol and ultrapure water (1:1 v/v). The solution was then transferred to a Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 180 °C for 12 h. After reaction, the product was naturally cooled down to room temperature, transferred to a 50.0 mL centrifuge tube and subjected to centrifugation at 10000 rpm for 15 min to remove large particles. The supernatant containing CDs was collected and dialyzed against 1.0 L ultrapure water through a dialysis membrane with molecular weight cut-off = 500–1000 Da (Shanghai Yuanye Biotechnology Co., Ltd, Shanghai, China) for 3 days, during which the ultrapure water was recharged every 24 h. Finally, the purified CDs solution was freeze-dried to get fine powder for further use.

The transmission electron microscope (TEM) images of CDs were acquired with a CM100 electron microscope (Philips, Eindhoven, Netherlands) with an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. An Elementar Analysen systeme vario EL cube organic elemental analyzer (Hanau, Germany) was used for the elemental analysis. The Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) measurement of CDs was studied by a Varian 670-IR + 610-IR FTIR spectrometer (Lincolnshire, IL, USA). The ultraviolet and visible (UV–vis) spectrum was acquired on a UV-2550 absorption spectrophotometer (Shimadzu Co., Ltd., Tokyo, Japan). The PL spectrum of CDs was recorded on a RF-5301PC fluorescence spectrophotometer (Shimadzu Co., Ltd., Tokyo, Japan). The fluorescence decay curves were acquired on a FLS-980 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Edinburgh Co., Ltd., England).

To evaluate the linear detection range, a stock solution of CDs at a concentration of 0.3 mg mL−1 was prepared by dissolving 3.0 mg of the as-synthesized CDs in 10.0 mL PBS solution (pH 12.0). Then, 2.0 mL of the CDs solution was transferred into a quartz cuvette and different amounts of CGA were titrated with the final concentrations in a range of 0.08–110.0 μM. After being mixed together, the fluorescence intensity of the mixture solution was measured at an emission wavelength of 453 nm with an excitation wavelength of 350 nm. To calculate the detection limit, 3σ/slope rule was used with twelve repeated measurements of the CDs blank solution was performed.

To test the selectivity of the proposed method for CGA detection, a series of 2.0 mL of CDs solution (pH 12.0, 0.3 mg mL−1) were transferred into 5.0 mL centrifuge tubes. Then, 2.0 μL of various substances (100.0 mM), including CGA, cations (Fe2+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Zn2+, Ag2+ and Na+), anions (CO3 2–, SO4 2-, NO3 –, Cl-, H2PO4 -, S2O3 2-, Br-, I-, Cr2- and HPO4 2-), CA isomers (neochlorogenic acid, cryptochlorogenic acid, cynarin, isochlorogenic acid A, isochlorogenic acid B and isochlorogenic acid C) and several other small molecules (dl-methionine, β-hydroxyalanine, arginine, threonine, histidine, tyrosine, glycine, sucrose, glucose, d-fructose, ascorbic, leucine, glutamic acid, folic acid, tryptophan, aspartic, phenylalanine) were added, respectively. After handshaking to mix, the fluorescence intensities of the mixture solutions were recorded at an emission wavelength of 453 nm with an excitation wavelength of 350 nm.

To investigate the anti-interference ability of the proposed method for CGA detection, a series of 2.0 mL of CDs solution (pH 12.0, 0.3 mg mL−1) were transferred into 5.0 mL centrifuge tubes. Then, 2.0 μL of a CGA solution (100.0 mM) was added into each tube. Afterwards, 2.0 μL of the above-mentioned possible interferences (100.0 mM) were introduced into each tube, respectively. Finally, the fluorescence intensities of the mixture solutions were recorded in a similar way with the above selectivity evaluation.

The instant coffee, green coffee beans, roasted beans and honeysuckle were selected as food samples for analyses. In detail, the samples were dried in a vacuumed environment at 60 °C for overnight and then they were smashed to fine powder. 0.1 g of each beans powder and instant coffee were weighed and dissolved in 10.0 mL ultrapure water, respectively. Then, they were stirred and treated by ultra-sonication for half hour to assist dissolution. Afterwards, each sample solution was centrifuged for 15 min at 10,000 rpm and the supernatant were filtered through a polystyrene microfilter with 0.22 μM pore size. The filtrates were collected and kept in a refrigerator at 4 °C for further use.

In a typical procedure, a series of 2.0 mL CDs solution were transferred into 5.0 mL centrifuge tubes. Then, 20.0 μL of each sample filtrate was introduced and mixed by hand shaking. Then, the fluorescence intensities of CDs solutions without (F0) and with (F) the addition of sample filtrates were collected at an emission wavelength of 453 nm with an excitation wavelength of 350 nm, and the CGA content in food samples can be calculated with the assistance of linear curve. All measurements were carried out with five replicates. Additionally, different amounts of CGA were spiked into the filtrate of each food sample. The spiked CGA amount in honeysuckle sample filtrate were 3.0, 5.0 and 10.0 μM while that in instant coffee, green coffee beans, roasted beans filtrates were 5.0, 10.0 and 15.0 μM, respectively. To calculate the quantification recoveries, the total CGA amounts were determined in the same way as above.

ce:section.ce:section-title: CGA determination in food samples by HPLC analysis
ce:section.ce:para: A 1200 series Agilent HPLC (Santa Clara, CA, USA) with a photodiode array detector was used for HPLC analyses of CGA in food samples. The UV detection wavelength was set at 368 nm, and the sample separation was completed by using a C18 column (150 × 4.6 mm id stainless steel) from Waters Corporation (Milford, MA, USA) with a mixture of 10.0 mM ammonium acetate and acetonitrile (60:40, v/v) as the mobile phase. An injection volume of 20.0 μL with a flow rate at 1.0 mL min−1 was used for CGA quantification.

All experiments were run in quintuplicate, and the results were reported as mean ± standard deviation. The line charts and bar diagrams were prepared using Origin 2021 (OriginLab, Northampton, MA, USA).Statistical analysis was carried out using pair t-test with SPSS software version 23.0 (IBM, USA). Differences were considered statistically significant when the significance test value (P) was less than 0.05.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical and structural characterization of CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The TEM was applied to gain an idea about the particle size and morphology of CDs. As displayed in Fig. 1 A, the CDs species were spherical in shape and mostly monodispersed on the TEM image. The particle size distribution histogram illustrated in Fig. 1A showed that the average particle size of the CDs species was estimated to be 5.5 ± 0.3 nm.

Elemental analysis was used to get the information about the composition information of CDs. The CDs were composed of C 48.08 %, H 10.17 %, O 35.07 % and N 6.68 %. The surface structure of CDs was investigated by FTIR in Fig. S1. The absorption band at 3000–3460 cm−1 was ascribed to the stretching vibrations of –OH and N H. The peak at 2600 cm−1 was related to CH stretching vibration, and the absorption peaks at 1900 cm−1, 1720–1520 cm−1, 1200 cm−1 and 1000–800 cm−1 were corresponding to CO, CC, CN and CO respectively. With this information, the CDs were revealed to be functionalized with –COOH, –OH, and –NH2 groups, which afford good water solubility of CDs, which is beneficial to the sensing work in aqueous environment.

The spectral characteristics of CDs were evaluated by the UV–vis absorption and photoluminescence (PL) spectroscopy. Fig. 1B displayed the UV–vis absorption spectrum of CDs. It can be seen that the CDs displayed two typical absorption bands at 245 nm and 352 nm. The absorption peak at 245 nm was attributed to the π-π* transition of the aromatic sp2 (Jiao et al., 2018) and the peak at 352 nm were assigned to n-π* transition of the CO bond (Hu et al., 2021). Fig. 1C displayed the PL spectra of CDs under different excitation wavelength. When the excitation wavelength increased from 310 nm to 450 nm, the emission wavelength of CDs changed from 453 nm to 557 nm, indicating an excitation wavelength-dependent PL behaviour. The existence of multiple emission sites were believed to be due to the size effect and the presence of different functionalities on the CDs surface (Fang, Li, Wang, Wu, & Wang, 2018; Liu et al., 2012). In addition, the highest emission intensity was obtained at an excitation wavelength of 350 nm. Upon excitation under a UV lamp, the CDs solution emitted yellow fluorescence. The quantum yield (QY) of the CDs (Fig. S2) was calculated to be 3.7 % with quinine sulfate as the reference standard.

As well known that, the concentration of CDs, pH and reaction time are critical detection parameters for analytical purposes. The changes of fluorescence quenching efficiency (F0/F) with the CDs concentration were firstly monitored. Fig. S3A showed the plots of F0/F against the concentration of CDs. It clearly showed that when the CDs concentration increased from 0.01 to 0.3 mg mL−1, the F0/F gradually increased and a further increase of CDs concentration (0.3–1.0 mg mL−1) resulted in continuous decrease of F0/F. Since the highest F0/F occurred when the dosage of CDs was 0.3 mg mL−1, 0.3 mg mL−1 was used as the optimal CDs concentration for further studies.

Then, the influence of pH on the quenching effects was studied and the results are displayed in Fig. S3B. It can be seen that varying pH in a range of 2.0–11.0 resulted in unobvious changes of F0/F. By contrast, the pH 12.0 lead to a sharp increase of F0/F, which may due to the oxidation of CDs surface at a high alkaline condition. As such, pH 12.0 was selected as the optimal sensing condition for further studies.

In addition, the changes of F0/F with reaction time were monitored and the results are shown in Fig. S3C. It can be seen that upon the CDs was mixed with CGA, the F0/F increased drastically within 1.0 min and remained fairly constant by further lengthening the reaction time. This indicated that only 1.0 min was needed to complete the reaction between CDs and CGA. Based on these observations, 1.0 min was selected as the optimal reaction time for CGA determination.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence quenching of CDs by CGA
ce:section.ce:para: For purpose of exploring the response of CDs towards CGA, the fluorescence signals of CDs in the presence of different amounts of CGA were recorded. As shown in Fig. 2 A, the fluorescence intensity of CDs showed a CGA concentration-dependent behavior, which weakened gradually with the increase of CGA concentration. The fluorescence quenching efficiency, i.e., F0/F, was found to have good linear relationship with the concentration of CGA within two ranges of 0.01–0.1 μM and 0.1–20.0 μM in Fig. 2B and Fig. 2C, respectively. The fitted linear regression equation is y = 0.946C + 1.073 and y = 0.0698C + 1.161, respectively, where C is the CGA concentration. The linear range of CGA was from 0.01 to 0.1 μM and 0.1 to 20.0 μM with LODs of 8.87 nM and 0.12 μM.

The linear range and LOD of the proposed method was compared with that of the previously reported CDs-based fluorescence methods and the other existing analytical methods. As shown in Table S2, the LOD of our proposed method is 2 orders of magnitude lower than that of the reported CDs-based fluorescence methods and is even 1–3 orders of magnitude lower than that of HPLC-UV, voltammetry and amperometric photosensory methods, indicating that our proposed method was of ultrahigh sensitivity. Additionally, our proposed method had other merits of low cost, ease of operation and fast response.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Response mechanism of CDs to CGA
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence and UV–vis absorption spectroscopy were firstly used to study the mechanism of CDs response to CGA. As shown in Fig. 3 A, the absorption spectrum of CGA (absorber) precisely overlapped with the excitation spectrum of CDs (fluorophore) and partially coincided with the emission spectrum of CDs. This indicates that the quenching mechanism should be resulted from IFE (Fang et al., 2018). In addition, a fluorescence quenching phenomenon can be generally clarified as dynamic quenching or static quenching where the former one refers to an electron transfer process and the later one refers to the formation of a radiation-free complex (Huang et al., 2015; Liu et al., 2012). The fluorescence lifetime of CDs in the absence and in the presence of CGA were recorded (Fig. 3B). The fitted results are shown in Table S3. The average fluorescence lifetime of CDs was 3.07 ns and decreased to 2.63 ns after mixing with CGA. The reduction in fluorescence life time indicated that the interaction between CDs and CGA was a dynamic process. In summary, the quenching of CDs fluorescence by CGA was due to a combined effect of IFE and dynamic quenching process.

Selectivity and anti-interference ability are important characteristics for a promising nanoprobe. To explore whether the proposed fluorescence method was specific for CGA detection, the fluorescence signals in the presence of CGA and the potentially interfering components (cations, anions, CA isomers and the other small molecules) in real food samples were recorded under the same conditions, respectively. As shown in Fig. 4 A, the CDs have no obvious response to the possible interferences, and on the contrary, the introduction of CGA caused a remarkable decrease in the fluorescence intensity of CDs. The results clearly indicated that the proposed method had good sensing selectivity for CGA detection.

Meanwhile, the anti-interference ability of the proposed method was studied. The fluorescence intensities of the CDs/CGA system with the existence of the above-mentioned possible interferences were measured. As illustrated in Fig. 4B, the fluorescence intensity of the CDs/CGA system did not change significantly upon mixing with these possible interfering species, which imply that the CDs-based fluorescence method exhibited high anti-interference ability for CGA detection.

To evaluate the feasibility of the proposed fluorescence method for practical application, the established sensing platform was used for CGA determination in real food samples. The analyses results are displayed in Table 1 . According to the two linear equations, the contents of CGA in the above-mentioned samples were determined to be 7.50 μM (honeysuckle), 0.85 μM (green coffee beans), 0.08 μM (roasted beans), 0.02 μM (instant coffee), respectively. The quantitative recoveries were obtained to evaluate the accuracy of the proposed method. As shown by Table 1, the recovery values were obtained in a range of 98.9–106.7 %, and the relative standard deviations (RSDs) were less than 3.28 %, indicating that the proposed method is accurate for CGA detection. In addition, HPLC analyses were implemented to further check the accuracy of the proposed approach. The HPLC chromatograms and linearity plots were depicted in Fig. S4. The t test (Table S4) was carried out by using a SPSS 21.0 software. It was shown that the results obtained by the two methods do not have significant difference, indicating that the results achieved by our work are in good agreement with that obtained by HPLC analyses, which unambiguously demonstrated that the CDs-based sensing platform is capable of detecting CGA in real food commodities with high accuracy.

In summary, the CDs from hydrothermal treating of citric acid and p-pda were proposed as fluorescence nanoprobe for CGA detection. The proposed method was of low cost and exhibited good selectivity, ultrahigh sensitivity and accuracy for CGA analyses. Moreover, the detection process exhibited rapid, simple and convenient characteristics. The feasibility of proposed method for real applications was validated by using the CDs nanoprobe for CGA determination in real food samples. This method certainly provides a convenient analytical strategy with favourable reliability for CGA detection, which is expected to have wide application prospect for CGA monitoring in the fields of food safety and food management.

Qin Hu: Funding acquisition, Methodology, Formal analysis, Data curation, Writing – original draft. Wenhui Wang: Data curation. Zhen-quan Yang: Formal analysis. Lixia Xiao: Formal analysis. Xiaojuan Gong: Methodology. Lizhen Liu: Validation. Jie Han: Funding acquisition, Methodology, Supervision, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The financial supports from Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province, China (BK20200949), China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (2020M671625), Postdoctoral Research Funding Program of Jiangsu Province (2021K249B), National Natural Science Foundation of China (21922202), Natural Science Fund for Colleges and Universities in Jiangsu Province, China (19KJB150042) are gratefully acknowledged.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.foodchem.2022.134395.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: Alpar et al., 2018

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Selective and simultaneous determination of total chlorogenic acids, vanillin and caffeine in foods and beverages by adsorptive stripping voltammetry using a cathodically pretreated boron-doped diamond electrode
Sensors and Actuators B: Chemical

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Alpar, N., Yardım, Y., & Şentürk, Z. (2018). Selective and simultaneous determination of total chlorogenic acids, vanillin and caffeine in foods and beverages by adsorptive stripping voltammetry using a cathodically pretreated boron-doped diamond electrode. Sensors and Actuators B: Chemical, 257, 398-408. 10.1016/j.snb.2017.10.100.

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: Che et al., 2016

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Simultaneous qualitation and quantitation of chlorogenic acids in Kuding tea using ultra-high-performance liquid chromatography-diode array detection coupled with linear ion trap-Orbitrap mass spectrometer
Molecules

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Che, Y., Wang, Z., Zhu, Z., Ma, Y., Zhang, Y., Gu, W., Zhang, J., & Rao, G. (2016). Simultaneous Qualitation and Quantitation of Chlorogenic Acids in Kuding Tea Using Ultra-High-Performance Liquid Chromatography-Diode Array Detection Coupled with Linear Ion Trap-Orbitrap Mass Spectrometer. Molecules, 21(12). 10.3390/molecules21121728.

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: Cho et al., 2010

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Chlorogenic acid exhibits anti-obesity property and improves lipid metabolism in high-fat diet-induced-obese mice
Food and Chemical Toxicology

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Cho, A. S., Jeon, S. M., Kim, M. J., Yeo, J., Seo, K. I., Choi, M. S., & Lee, M. K. (2010).
